original_abstract: This study explores the theological and social perspectives of Ahmad Syarif Yahya regarding interreligious harmony in Indonesia, specifically focusing on his seminal works, "Ngaji Toleransi" and "Fikih Toleransi." As contemporary challenges increasingly blur the lines between private faith and public social interaction—often leading to religious ambiguity—this research seeks to clarify the Islamic framework for co-existence. Employing a descriptive-analytical approach through qualitative library research, the paper investigates how Yahya’s conceptualization of tolerance balances the preservation of individual creed (*aqidah*) with the necessity of social welfare (*maslahah*), brotherhood, and peace. The findings indicate that while Islam encourages robust social cooperation and mutual respect, there are definitive theological boundaries that must be maintained to safeguard the integrity of one’s faith. Ultimately, this research provides a practical framework for fostering communal harmony in a pluralistic society, contributing to a deeper understanding of how Indonesian Muslims can navigate religious diversity without compromising their core spiritual convictions.
humanized_abstract: Ahmad Syarif Yahya, as one of the Indonesian Muslim scholars who has done much to enhance inter-religious harmony in Indonesia, has written very effectively on this theme in his books entitled “Ngaji Toleransi” and “Fikih Toleransi”. In an age when religion and the public domain are increasingly merging, and religious differences are becoming increasingly obscured, so that the world becomes more ambiguous and less clear-cut, it is vital to understand the Islamic principles of living with others, who are not Muslims. Using a descriptive-analytical approach with qualitative library research, this paper seeks to discuss the Islamic principles of coexistence as put forward by Ahmad Syarif Yahya, through examining his theory of tolerance and its relationship to faith and social welfare, which is illustrated by the relationship between 'aqidah (individual creed) and maslahah (social welfare) in the context of brotherhood and peace. In Islam, as a religion, is expected to support a strong bond of cooperation and mutual respect among the people. It is however, important to note that there are also Islamic restrictions that must be upheld in order to safeguard the faith. Therefore, this paper will discuss a theory that is applicable to the realisation of togetherness in a pluralistic society, particularly for Muslims in Indonesia, in an effort to balance the realisation of faith with the necessities of life in a pluralistic society.
